2002-07-01.title: Scores Feared Dead as Two Jets Collide Over Southern Germany
2002-07-01.abstract: A Russian passenger airliner collided with a Boeing 757 cargo plane over southern Germany late Monday night, apparently killing everyone aboard both planes.

2002-07-01.title: Mexico Secrets: Envelope Holds Ghosts of 70's
2002-07-01.abstract: Luis Echeverria, Mexico's president from 1970 to 1976, may be called to testify under oath before special commission about role in country's never-acknowledged 'dirty war' from late 1960's and early 1980's in which hundreds of leftists were tortured and killed; his denial of wrongdoing will be tested as special prosecutor begins formal investigation of thousands of newly opened files from country's disbanded secret police; government's own human rights commission says at least 275 people, or perhaps twice that number, died during illegal detention; privately examined secret police files and interviewed hundreds of witnesses and former officials before turning over sealed list containing names of 74 former government officials it holds responsible to Pres Vicente Fox; photos (M)

2002-07-01.title: Without Fanfare or Cases, International Court Sets Up
2002-07-01.abstract: New International Criminal Court begins operations in The Hague over strong objections from Washington, ready to receive complaints about gross abuses of human rights anywhere in world; created by Treaty of Rome in 1998, court has jurisdiction over war crimes, crimes against humanity and genocide; organizers say it is likely to be year before first trial takes place (M)

2002-07-01.title: U.S. Vetoes Bosnia Mission, Then Allows 3-Day Reprieve
2002-07-01.abstract: US refuses to budge in its demand that American peacekeepers be exempted from new International Criminal Court by vetoing UN Security Council resolution extending peacekeeping mission in Bosnia, but later agrees to three-day extension; display of brinksmanship is sharpest demonstration to date of Bush administration's determination to preclude any possibility that new court, which formally comes into being on July 1, could be used to prosecute American peacekeepers; other members of Security Council, most notably Britain and France, have argued adamantly that court has ample safeguards against politically motivated prosecutions; have refused to allow council to alter rules to satisfy Washington; photo (M)

2002-07-01.title: Belgrade Journal; Serbia Expels a School for Teaching Corruption
2002-07-01.abstract: Serbia's Ministry of Education closes 11th Belgrade Grammar School, formerly prestigious high school, because of widespread corruption among teachers and staff; former principal Vera Ivkovic, whose anticorruption campaign attracted government's attention, says it will be difficult to clean up nation's education system, neglected for years under Slobodan Milosevic's violent decade in power unless young people can be convinced that money and connections are not only passports to success; photo (M)

2002-07-02.title: On an Anniversary, Chinese Leader Tells Hong Kong to Be Loyal
2002-07-02.abstract: Pres Jiang Zemin of China delivers tough speech in Hong Kong to mark fifth anniversary of its transfer from British to Chinese rule; urges residents to be more loyal to 'motherland' (S)

2002-07-02.title: Berlin Journal; Dear Euro, They Sigh (Not Fondly)
2002-07-02.abstract: Germans believe cost of living has increased ever since mark was replaced by euro, common collective currency of most of European Union, on January 1; embraced currency but were quickly embarrassed as it sank immediately below parity with dollar; few are heartened by fact that euro is again at rough parity; consider rise not function of euro's strength but dollar's weakness; photo (M)

2002-07-02.title: Migrants to Chinese Boom Town Find Hard Lives
2002-07-02.abstract: More than 100 million rural Chinese from Nanba area have migrated to cities, with more than half ending up in southern province of Guangdong and forming vast new urban underclass; seldom able to become legal residents, they are blamed for every social ill; many turn to sex trade as financial backstop, with their cheap labor fueling Guangdong's thriving economy, which accounts for half of China's gross domestic product; few reap any benefits, remaining unwanted outsiders for many years in strange and often hostile region; map; photo (M)

2002-07-02.title: Bolivian Congress to Decide Tight Presidential Election
2002-07-02.abstract: Bolivian voters, frustrated with economic turbulence and grinding poverty, are supporting unusually diverse array of politicians in presidential balloting on July 7, shaking up traditional politics in country's most important election in 20 years of democracy; exit surveys give Gonzalo Sanchez de Lozada, former president and pro-market reformer, razor-thin edge over Manfred Reyes Villa, populist former mayor of Cochabamba, country's third-largest city; voters also give unexpected support to Evo Morales, leftist leader of coca farmers who favors nationalizing private companies and often criticizes US for its involvement in Bolivian affairs (M)

2002-07-02.title: South Korea Wins Support From Japan On Sea Clash
2002-07-02.abstract: South Korean Pres Kim Dae Jung wins Japanese support for stronger policy on North Korea, and South Korean Defense Min Kim Dong Shin asks American military authorities for permission to strike first if threatened by intruding ships; June 29 clash between North Korean and South Korean patrol boats in Yellow Sea, which left four South Koreans and estimated 30 North Koreans dead discussed; shootout is second worst naval clash between both countries since end of Korean War 50 years ago (M)

2002-07-03.title: World Briefing | Europe: Czech Republic: Vandals Hit Jewish Sites
2002-07-03.abstract: Vandals attack synagogue and memorial to Holocaust victims in Czech Republic spa town of Karlovy Vary (S)

2002-07-03.title: Arafat Replaces 2 Security Chiefs
2002-07-03.abstract: Yasir Arafat shakes up security services, removing Jibril Rajoub in West Bank and Ghazi Jibali in Gaza; Rajoub is considered by some Israeli and American officials as among most cooperative Palestinian leaders (S)

2002-07-03.title: Balloonist, No Quitter, Circles the World Solo on His Sixth Attempt
2002-07-03.abstract: Steve Fossett prepares to land his balloon in southern Australia after completing first nonstop solo flight around the world without a motor; journey covers 19,400 miles, all in Southern Hemisphere; photo of control crew in St Louis; photos of balloons landing in three of Fossett's five previous failures (M)

2002-07-03.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Foot-And-Mouth Culprit Sentenced
2002-07-03.abstract: Robert Waugh, Northumberland farmer whose pigs are thought to have starr

2002-07-03.title: Shocked Afghans Criticize U.S. Strike; Toll Is Some 40 Dead and 100 Wounded
2002-07-03.abstract: Afghan government expresses dismay at reports that about 140 civilians were killed or wounded in US-led operation in south; Pres Hamid Karzai summons Lt Gen Dan K McNeill; White House expresses condolences, but Pentagon refuses to accept responsibility or explain fully what remains murky situation; officials say that Taliban might have used civilians as shields and that errant bomb from B-52 crashed into uninhabited hillside; gunship in same area attacked six locations after crew reported anti-aircraft fire; foreign minister Abdullah says about 40 people were killed, mostly at wedding in one of four villages hit; US flies four young children to military hospital; photo; troops have been looking for Taliban and Qaeda pockets in Dehrawud area, which includes home village of Mullah Muhammad Omar; US spokesman dismisses idea that firing was celebratory, saying crew felt aircraft was under sustained attack from ground (M)

2002-07-04.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-07-04.abstract: INDIA: New Tension Over Kashmir; SOUTH KOREA: After Battle, Phone Deal Is Off.

2002-07-04.title: Washington Allows a 12-Day Extension For U.N. Peacekeeping Mission in Bosnia
2002-07-04.abstract: US agrees to another extension of United Nations peacekeeping mission in Bosnia without provision placing American peacekeepers beyond reach of new International Criminal Court; Sec Gen Kofi Annan, in letter to Sec of State Colin N Powell, argues that sort of politically motivated prosecution US fears is highly improbable, that Americans are putting entire UN peacekeeping system at risk and that proposed American provision 'flies in the face of treaty law' (M)

2002-07-04.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: After Battle, Phone Deal Is Off
2002-07-04.abstract: Talks between two Koreas on opening mobile phone system in North's capital, Pyongyang, are suspended as result of June 29 naval shootout in Yellow Sea for which South blames North (S)

2002-07-04.title: World Briefing | Africa: Rwanda: Genocide Acquittal Upheld
2002-07-04.abstract: Appeals court of International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda upholds acquittal of Ignace Bagilisema, former mayor of Mabanza, on charges of playing leading role in mass killings of 1994 (S)

2002-07-04.title: Who won the Cup?Korea and Ronaldo
2002-07-04.abstract: Once the ball has stopped rolling, and the Brazilians stop partying, we can begin to count the cost and the gains of World Cup 2002.

2002-07-05.title: Some Indonesians Say U.S. Aid Could Promote Army Reforms
2002-07-05.abstract: Bush administration's effort to re-establish military ties with Indonesia, which has been curtailed for nearly decade because of its army's widespread human rights violations, has some unexpected support among Indonesians; many who suffered under military in past, and who are still critical of army for its continuing rights abuses, contend that best hope for eventually developing army whose conduct is appropriate for democracy is to send officers to schools in United States (M)

2002-07-05.title: Pressure to Plan for War
2002-07-05.abstract: The pressure on the Pentagon to plan for a war on Iraq underscores the failure of diplomacy and covert operations to dislodge Saddam Hussein. News analysis, Page A6.

2002-07-05.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-07-05.abstract: NORTH KOREA: Warm Words Follow Bullets; INDONESIA: Taking Aim At Rebels;.

2002-07-05.title: Israelis and Palestinians Settle Further Into Their Stalemate
2002-07-05.abstract: Israel's outgoing army chief of staff said today that only the expulsion or replacement of the Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat might prompt an Israeli withdrawal from West Bank cities.

2002-07-05.title: World Briefing: Africa
2002-07-05.abstract: TUNISIA: Trial For Attack Suspect'S Uncle; MALAWI: President Will Need New Job; NIGERIA: Crime-Fighting Aid From U.S.;.

2002-07-06.title: World Briefing | Africa: Malawi: Term Limit Remains In Place
2002-07-06.abstract: Malawi Parliament votes against eliminating term limit preventing Pres Bakili Muluzi from seeking third term; photo (S)

2002-07-06.title: Files Bare Dark Secrets of Mexico's Dirty War
2002-07-06.abstract: Move by Mexican Pres Vicente Fox to open to public vast archive of secret goverment security documents is providing catharsis to families of victims of 1970's 'dirty war' and sparking investigations of former presidents and cabinet ministers; many relatives of dead and missing remain skeptical that probes will lead to convictions of high-ranking officials; Martha Murillo de Gaxiola, whose 24-year-old son, Oscar, disappeared in February 1978, comments; photo; map (M)

2002-07-06.title: World Briefing | The Americas: Brazil: Journalist's Remains Recovered
2002-07-06.abstract: Charred remains of Brazilian crime reporter Tim Lopes, who disappeared June 2 during undercover investigation into gangs that dominate squatter slums in Rio de Janeiro, are recovered from clandestine cemetery; Lopes reportedly was executed by local drug boss nicknamed Elias the Madman; photo (S)

2002-07-06.title: Longtime Madagascar Leader Flees as Rival Gains
2002-07-06.abstract: Longtime Madagascar leader Didier Ratsiraka flees with family to Seychelles Island as hostile troops close in on last remaining stronghold; Ratsiraka has been battling rival Marc Ravalomanana since bitterly contested December 2001 election; US and France have officially recognized Ravalomanana as president (M)

2002-07-06.title: Former Nazi Convicted of Ordering Executions
2002-07-06.abstract: Former SS officer Friedrich Engel, 93, is convicted in Hamburg of ordering killing of 59 Italian prisoners in May 1944; Judge Rolf Seedorf sentences Engel to seven years in prison but he is released pending results of health tests (M)

2002-07-07.title: Afghan Official Is Assassinated; Blow to Karzai
2002-07-07.abstract: Hajji Abdul Qadir, Afghan vice president, is assassinated in Kabul, and stability of Hamid Karzai's American-backed government is thrown into question; Qadir and his driver are shot outside his office by gunmen lurking nearby; gunmen escape and police, suspecting conspiracy, detain Qadir's entourage of bodyguards and seal off Kabul's main streets; Qadir, longtime warlord from eastern Afghanistan, was pivotal figure in government, largely because he was one of few Pashtuns to join Karzai in effort to unite country's major ethnic groups; government is dominated by minority Tajiks; photos (M)

2002-07-07.title: Europe Calls It Vital Tax Data; Swiss Banks Call It Vitally Secret
2002-07-07.abstract: Switzerland is resisting pressure from other European governments to ferret out tax cheats, fearing it would frighten capital out of their banks; controversy has become public, with European ministers regularly rebuking Swiss for failing to sign European financial disclosure agreement; accord could fail entirely without Swiss involvement because country's banks control about $27 trillion of world's funds and handle third of all money transferred across European borders; 15-country European Union insists that Switzerland help fight tax evasion--though that is not crime in Switzerland--by disclosing amount of interest paid to nonresidents so they can be taxed accordingly in their home countries (M)

2002-07-07.title: U.N. Coaxes Out the Wheres and Whys of Global Immigration
2002-07-07.abstract: United Nations population division will meet in New York to begin exploring global cooperation on issue of immigration statistics; demographers say that past attempts to analyze population movements have been frustrated by govenrment officials who try to inflate or to obscure them; now there is intensifying debate about immigration in Europe and US, partly fueled by fears of terrorists crossing borders; beyond what are perceived as problems of immigration in richer countries, there is mounting evidence of threats to peace and life from migrations, often forced, in poor nations (M)

2002-07-07.title: Iran's Students Step Up Reform Drive
2002-07-07.abstract: Forceful student movement is replacing Pres Mohammad Khatami as leading engine for political reform in Iran; activists at country's vibrant, overflowing universities say their calls for change are gathering momentum and they are preparing to put delicate issues like renewing relations with United states to public vote for first time; Khatami has twice been elected president but has been stymied in his quest for change by still-powerful conservative clerics and their supporters; unlike him, students are not patient, polite or at all ready to settle for status quo; they are aware of realities outside Iran because of Internet and satellite television; they read about Western democracy and are taught by professors who studied in West; photo (M)

2002-07-07.title: Greek Police Find Another Arms Cache in 2nd Raid of Week
2002-07-07.abstract: Greek police intensify hunt for members of country's November 17 terror group; discover second huge weapons cache, including anti-tank missiles and explosives in residential apartment block in central Athens; United States Embassy goes on maximum alert to guard against retaliation from group, which is blamed for at least 23 killings, including 1975 assassination of Richard Welch, CIA's station chief in Athens; police win biggest breakthrough in 27 years with arrest last week of foot soldier of terror group during botched bomb attack at port of Pireaus; suspect is said to be providing information to authorities about group (M)

2002-07-08.title: Despite Peace Hopes, 27 Hurt in Ulster Protestant Parade
2002-07-08.abstract: Parade by Protestant Orange Order in Portadown, Northern Ireland, ends in heated scuffles that injure 27 people, despite predictions by organizers and security forces that annual event would be peaceful for first time in nearly decade; crowds of Orangemen and Protestant supporters dismantle steel barricades blocking them from marching through predominantly Roman Catholic neighborhood and clash with police; photo (M)

2002-07-08.title: For Israelis Wounded in Bomb Attacks, Recovery Is a Battle
2002-07-08.abstract: Article on physical and emotional trauma caused by suicide bombings and long ordeal facing Israelis gravely wounded in attacks; wounds are often severe, but not just physical; more than 4,000 Israelis have been injured since conflict with Palestinians began anew in September 2000, almost 10 times roughly 500 killed; among Palestinians, at least 1,450 have been killed and, according to Palestinian Authority, another 35,700 wounded; Palestinians say they are being injured and killed in larger numbers in course of resisting military occupation; Israelis say more of their own victims are noncombatants, women and children who are often killed or wounded in suicide bombings; in small nation like Israel, wounded produce ripple effect through society that partly explains strong support Israelis give military measures like recent retaking of practically entire West Bank to arrest militants and curb suicide bombings; wounded go on, publicly and painfully, battling wounds often far worse than those seen in nation at peace; recovery, when it comes, is almost always accompanied by ample parts of fear and anger; physical and mental trauma caused by bombings described; photos (L)

2002-07-08.title: Controller Sent Jets Into Crash, Flight Data Show
2002-07-08.abstract: Data from the flight recorders aboard the two jets that collided over Germany last week show that a Swiss air traffic controller inadvertently put the planes on a collision course.

2002-07-08.title: Afghan Killing May Be Linked to Drug Trade
2002-07-08.abstract: Afghans mourn slain Vice President Hajji Abdul Qadir as officials investigate possibility that he was killed by drug lords double-crossed during Western-backed campaign to destroy country's poppy crop; he was overseeing Western-financed campaign begun in April to destroy fields; Afghan officials have been paying farmers about $500 per acre to destroy poppy plants (M)

2002-07-08.title: Kurds, Secure in North Iraq Zone, Are Wary About a U.S. Offensive
2002-07-08.abstract: Bush adminstration's stated goal of toppling regime of Iraq Pres Saddam Hussein runs into strong reservations from Iraqi Kurdish leaders, who would be crucial allies in any military campaign; leaders flatly say they will be reluctant to join American military operations that put Kurds at risk of onslaught by Iraqi troops of kind they suffered after Persian Gulf War in 1991; even though American-led military campaign to topple Hussein would probably make Kurds freedom more secure, Kurdish leaders say Washington would be asking them to put all they have gained from decade of autonomy at risk of fresh Iraqi offensive; map (M)

2002-07-09.title: World Briefing | Europe: Yugoslavia: Serb Convicted Of War Crime
2002-07-09.abstract: Ivan Nikolic, former Yugoslav Army reservist, is sentenced to eight years in prison in Serbia's first war crimes trial for killing two Kosovo Albanian brothers in 1999 during Yugoslavia-NATO conflict (S)

2002-07-09.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Egypt: Pharaoh Heading Home
2002-07-09.abstract: Museum of Emory University will reportedly return to Egypt mummy believed to be Ramses I, pharaoh who ruled in 14th century BC (S)

2002-07-09.title: Disclosure by Merck is latest scandal ahead of president's speech : A new jolt as Bush heads to Wall Street
2002-07-09.abstract: WASHINGTON:—  As President George W. Bush prepared a get-tough speech on the scandals buffeting corporate America and two former top executives of WorldCom Inc. refused to answer questions in Congress, investors got another jolt Monday as the drugmaker Merck & Co. revealed that one of its subsidiaries had overstated its revenues by billions of dollars.

2002-07-09.title: World Briefing | Asia: Indonesia: Soldiers Said To Smuggle Rare Birds
2002-07-09.abstract: Animal Conservaton for Life accuses Indonesian soldiers of smuggling endangered birds from eastern provinces of Maluku and Papua (S)

2002-07-09.title: World Briefing | Africa: Rwanda: Tribunal Tries To Cut Backlog
2002-07-09.abstract: International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda agrees to hand over some of its cases to national courts in effort to clear backlog and wind up its work by 2008 (S)

2002-07-10.title: Senate Panel to Ask Bush Aides To Give Details on His Iraq Policy
2002-07-10.abstract: Senate Foreign Relations Committee plans hearings to question Bush administration officials on Iraq policy; Sen Joseph Biden calls for full public airing of how goal of removing Saddam Hussein from office might be accomplished (S)

2002-07-10.title: A Passing Fancy: A Bar Named for Putin
2002-07-10.abstract: Bar called Putin in Chelyabinsk, 900 miles east of Moscow, is closed three weeks after it was opened by two college students; photo; Pres Vladimir Putin, who does not seem comfortable with such tributes, apparently was not happy with use of his name (S)

2002-07-10.title: Demonstrators, Ignoring a Ban, Clash With Riot Police in Tehran
2002-07-10.abstract: Iranian protesters clash with police at Tehran University and in affluent neighborhood in demonstrations marking third anniversary of violent crackdown on student protests (S)

2002-07-10.title: An Inquiry Confirms a Massacre Of Jews by Poles in World War II
2002-07-10.abstract: Polish historians confirm that massacre of Jews in Jedwabne in 1941 was carried out by Poles, not occupying Germans; Radislaw Ignatiew presents conclusions of inquiry into findings of Polish-American historian Jan T Gross, whose book Neighbors told how Poles burned alive as many as 1,600 Jewish Poles; says at least 40 Poles carried out attack; 12 people were convicted in 1949 of aiding Germans (S)

2002-07-10.title: Prigorodnoye Journal; Gas May Soon Replace Kelp as Sakhalin's Mainstay
2002-07-10.abstract: All that is left of Prigorodnoye is three families and the ruins of a kelp processing plant.    The village is a dot on a green stretch of coast on the island of Sakhalin, off Russia's eastern coast. It began losing population several decades ago, but the process accelerated in the last 15 years as economic collapse engulfed Russia.

2002-07-11.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: U.S. Can Search For Downed Pilots
2002-07-11.abstract: China, in tentative move to re-establish exchanges with United States military, says it will let American military officials come to China to search for bodies of two pilots shot down near China-Korea border during Korean War (S)

2002-07-11.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-07-11.abstract: BRITAIN: Big Day For Great Robber; RUSSIA: Another Sign Explodes;.

2002-07-11.title: World Briefing | Americas: Brazil: Justice Minister Quits
2002-07-11.abstract: Brazilian Justice Min Miguel Reale Jr resigns to protest solicitor general's ruling against federal intervention to replace crime-ridden government of Espirito Santo State; police director Itanor Carneiro and other senior officials have also resigned to protest decision, which overturned recommendation made by Human Rights Council after lawyers combatting corrupt officials in state were assassinated (S)

2002-07-11.title: World Briefing: Asia
2002-07-11.abstract: PAKISTAN: Verdict In Pearl Case Due Soon; MALAYSIA: Conviction Upheld; CHINA: U.S. Can Search For Downed Pilots; INDIA: Counting Infiltrators; SOUTH KOREA: Ministers Replaced;.

2002-07-11.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: Counting Infiltrators
2002-07-11.abstract: India says infiltration of Islamic militants from Pakistani into Indian-controlled Kashmir dropped 30 percent between May 27 and June 23, but rose in last week of June (S)

2002-07-12.title: Diplomats Say Court Immunity Solution May Be in Sight
2002-07-12.abstract: Security Council holds several hours of closed-door debate about new American proposal to keep peacekeepers outside reach of International Criminal Court; diplomats say there is fair chance that divisive issue could be resolved soon; without resolution, mandate of Bosnian peacekeeping mission will soon expire, followed by other missions as they come up for renewal; issue has evolved in nasty dispute between United States and some of its closest allies, all of which have embraced new court (M)

2002-07-12.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-07-12.abstract: ITALY: New Immigration Law; VATICAN: Contender For The Papacy; RUSSIA: Novelist Faces Pornography Charge; SWITZERLAND: Two Quit In Scandal Over Scandal; BRITAIN: A Break For American Pets;.

2002-07-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Novelist Faces Pornography Charge
2002-07-12.abstract: Moscow investigators, acting on complaint from youth group that backs Pres Vladimir V Putin, charges Vladimir Sorokin with pornography over his 1999 novel Blue Lard, that depicts homosexual act between clones of Stalin and Khrushchev; free-speech advocates express alarm; government official calls charge misguided (S)

2002-07-12.title: World Briefing: Americas
2002-07-12.abstract: CUBA: Elections, But No Referendum; BOLIVIA: U.S. Warns Against Candidate;.

2002-07-12.title: A Few Saudis Defy a Rigid Islam To Debate Their Own Intolerance
2002-07-12.abstract: Cautious debate, prompted by Sept 11 terrorist attacks, is taking place in Saudi Arabia's closed society over intolerance toward non-Muslims and attitudes toward West that are now viewed by some as inspiring unacceptable violence; debate appears to represent significant shift in society whose Wahhabi branch of Islam tends to make such questioning taboo; small group of intellectuals, academics, journalists and religious scholars are quietly suggesting that change is needed, but such positions remain controversial; after scores of religius scholars and academics issued manifesto this spring suggesting that Muslims might find common ground with West, they were subjected to withering denunciations by those who accept Wahhabi notion that Islam thrives on hostility toward infidels; austere teachings of Muhammad bin Abd al-Wahhab have been prevalent in Saudi Arabia for more than two centuries, and ruling Saud dynasty owes its very control over peninsula's once fractious tribes to fact that their ancestors championed his teachings; Saudis in general, and senior princes in particular, reject notion that Wahhabi teachings spawn terrorism; photos (M)

2002-07-13.title: U.N. Ends Wrangle Over U.S. Immunity
2002-07-13.abstract: UN Security Council, ending unusual wrangle between US and most other members, unanimously adopts resolution effectively giving US peacekeepers year's exemption from prosecution by new International Criminal Court (M)

2002-07-13.title: In Ecuador's Banana Fields, Child Labor Is Key to Profits
2002-07-13.abstract: Children and adults, in interviews, discuss child labor at Los Alamos banana plantation near Puerto Inca, Ecuador; plantation, which employs some 1,300 workers, is owned by Alvaro Noboa, country's richest man and leading presidential candidate; growers and exporters in Ecuador, which supplies 25 percent of bananas exported to US, say product earns them about 30 percent less than decade ago, often prompting to turn blind eye to labor codes; photos; map and chart of child labor in Latin America; photos (M)

2002-07-13.title: Mexican Farmers Seize Officials
2002-07-13.abstract: About 16 people reportedly are injured and dozen others are arrested as hundreds of armed Mexican farmers seize control of government offices in San Salvador Atenco, abduct several local officials and demand that government abandon plans to take land for $2.3-billion international airport (M)

2002-07-13.title: U.S. Peacekeepers Given Year's Immunity From New Court
2002-07-13.abstract: UN Security Council, ending weeks-long confrontation with intense closed-door negotiations, unanimously adopts resolution that effectively gives American peacekeepers year's exemption from prosecution by new International Criminal Court; also renews mandates of UN peacekeeping missions in Bosnia and Prevlaka, Croatia (M)

2002-07-14.title: 9 Europeans Among 12 Hurt By Grenade Attack in Pakistan
2002-07-14.abstract: Handmade grenade slightly wounds 12 people, including 9 European tourists, in Pakistan's North-West Frontier Province; westerners have been targets of several attacks in recent months, with Islamic militants angry about American military operations in Afghanistan; deatils of latest attack are sketchy (M)

2002-07-14.title: F.B.I. AND MILITARY UNITE IN PAKISTAN TO HUNT AL QAEDA
2002-07-14.abstract: Pakistan has become laboratory for how American power could be used to combat terror; American law enforcement agencies are working with American military in Pakistan in hunt for Al Qaeda fighers who fled sanctuaries in Afghanistan and hope to revive their group; FBI agents are helping local police in Pakistani cities to break up what senior American intelligence and law enforcement officials regard as depleted but still dangerous network that carried out deadly attacks against Westerners in Karachi and Islamabad in recent weeks; never before have traditionally independent military and law enforcement organizations worked so much in concert; similar, if smaller, American operations appear to be unfolding in Philippines and Yemen; photos; map (L)

2002-07-14.title: Bangladeshis Sipping Arsenic As Plan for Safe Water Stalls
2002-07-14.abstract: Tens of thousands of Bangladeshis show outward signs of arsenic poisoning from their drinking water; World Health Orgn calls this largest mass poisoning of population in history; about 35 million Bangladeshis drink arsenic-contaminated water from tube wells that tap into underground aquifers; government, Unicef and various aid groups had encouraged installation of such wells, eager to wean nation from pond water which is often incubator for lethal disease; underground aquifers were never tested for arsenic until hundreds of people fell sick; in 1998, World Bank lent government $32.4 million to test every tube well and to provide safe sources of water; project has been hobbled by unforeseen problems, and most of country's estimated 11 million wells have yet to be tested; trusting Bangladeshi people are now told that water from 'safe' tube wells may be delivering arsenic-induced cancer; photos; maps (L)

2002-07-14.title: With Potential in the Air, Iraqi Exiles Meet
2002-07-14.abstract: Former Iraqi military officers who have defected during Saddam Hussein's 23 years in power meet in London to discuss ways of helping to bring him down; they are prompted by Pres Bush's calls for his overthrow; organizers say meeting has aim of sending message to military officers in Iraq, encouraging them to prepare to support American-led effort to oust Hussein; several of former officers claim Iraqi officers corps is rife with hatred for Hussein and could be persuaded to join coup or invading force, provided they are guaranteed future in post-Hussein Iraq; American officials from several agencies attend as observers; surprise guest at meeting is Prince Hassan bin-Talal of Jordan, brother of late King Hussein and heir to his throne until King changed succession in favor his his son, present King Abdullah; Abdullah's government quickly denies reports that American military will be allowed to use Jordan as base for air and ground attacks against Iraq; Prince Hassan claims his presence at meeting is personal; photo (M)

2002-07-14.title: Court Rules That Ontario Must Recognize Same-Sex Marriages
2002-07-14.abstract: Provincial court rules that Ontario government must recognize right of gays and lesbians to marry people of their own gender; ruling comes in response to lawsuit filed by lesbian couple and gay male couple to compel provincial government to register their marriage ceremonies as legally binding

2002-07-15.title: The Factory Worker; A Workplace Shifts As a Society Changes
2002-07-15.abstract: China's roughly 60 million factory workers, most of whom are still employed by the state, make up the bulk of the urban work force. As the worst of the state-owned enterprises are shut down, many of these people are being thrown out of work. Accustomed to the security of the state economy and concentrated enough to organize, they represent the most volatile slice of the population.    Tang Nianshan and his wife, Tang Airong, have spent most of their lives working in state-owned factories. Ms. Tang, 43, has been working for the same factory since she was 18. It makes enameled tin washbasins. She earns just under 1,000 yuan a month.

2002-07-15.title: India Voices Anger at Kashmir Raid That Killed 27
2002-07-15.abstract: Death toll from July 13 attack by guerrillas on Kashmir shantytown climbs to 27; Indian officials express outrage, but government avoids accusations while preparing official response; no one has claimed reponsibility (S)

2002-07-15.title: The Migrant; Building a New Life In an Unfriendly City
2002-07-15.abstract: Chen Qun, 28, is one of an estimated 150 million peasants who have left the land to find work in China's swelling cities. Most cities have large populations of these struggling ''outsiders,'' as urban residents call them.    City dwellers regard the migrants with suspicion and regularly blame them for rising crime. The migrants overwhelm railroads and bus lines early each year during an annual migration back to their hometowns for the all-important lunar New Year holiday. But their remittances to their families in the countryside remain the most significant means of distributing the wealth of China's cities and its coastal export belt to the vast, impoverished hinterland.

2002-07-15.title: Chirac Gunman Committed to Psychiatric Hospital
2002-07-15.abstract: A neo-Nazi gunmen who is accused of trying to kill President Jacques Chirac during Sunday's Bastille Day parade was committed to a psychiatric hospital today.

2002-07-15.title: China Juggles Conflicting Pressures of Society in Transition
2002-07-15.abstract: The Times interviewed people from different walks of life to give a sense of who the Chinese are today.

2002-07-16.title: Beijing Journal; Here She Comes! (Will China Ever Be the Same?)
2002-07-16.abstract: Beijing Journal column on struggle of 20-year old Zhuo Ling, who holds Miss China title, to win approval in country where beauty pageants are officially regarded as subversive; she placed third in Miss Universe contest, which she entered 'unofficially;' photos (M)

2002-07-16.title: Flurry Precedes Mideast Meeting in New York
2002-07-16.abstract: Israeli-Palestinian crisis causes flurry of diplomatic maneuvering on eve of high-level meeting in New York City at request of Sec of State Colin L Powell; meeting between representatives from US, UN, European Union and Russia faces central issue of future of Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, long embodiment of Palestinian aspirations who Pres Bush and advisers say cannot lead any future Palestinian state; Israeli Defense Min Benjamin Ben-Eliezer meets with Egyptian Pres Hosni Mubarak in Alexandria; both reportedly arrive at different conclusions about Arafat's future (M)

2002-07-16.title: An Icelandic Battle of Wildlife Versus Voltage
2002-07-16.abstract: Focus on controversy surrounding Karahnjukar Hydropower Project planned by Iceland's government to dam glacial river flows in large tract of wilderness and produce hydroelectric power for aluminium smelter planned by Alcoa; smelter will rest on country's wind-swept eastern fjords, with view to creating 2,000 construction jobs and 600 to 1,000 permanent ones in region Icelanders are deserting in droves; outraged environmentalists say country is selling its wild birthright, damaging its eco-tourist image and risking credit rating to benefit $23 billion American conglomerate and to win mere handful of jobs; backers of project say country has profitably taken big risks to attract smelters twice before, that it must keep its rural areas populated and that it will still be able to establish stunning national park, as environmentalists desire; photos; maps (M)

2002-07-16.title: I.R.A. Apologizes for Civilian Deaths
2002-07-16.abstract: The Irish Republican Army issued a straight, unmitigated apology today for the deaths of civilians killed in its violent efforts to unite Northern Ireland with the Irish Republic.

2002-07-16.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Journalist Ordered Out
2002-07-16.abstract: British newspaper Guardian's Zimbabwe correspondent Andrew Meldrum is acquitted on charges of publishing false information; he is ordered to leave country within 24 hours; photo (S)

2002-07-17.title: But Greenspan warns scandals are 'destructive to capitalism' : Fed chief's outlook upbeat
2002-07-17.abstract: With global financial markets on tenterhooks, Alan Greenspan, the chairman of the Federal Reserve Board, delivered a guardedly optimistic outlook Tuesday for the U.S. economy, saying "the fundamentals are in place for a return to sustained healthy growth."

2002-07-17.title: Arafat's Role Is in Dispute During Talks in New York
2002-07-17.abstract: Sec Colin Powell and envoys from UN, European Union and Russia meet in New York and agree on broad goal of creating Palestinian state within three years, but differ sharply on almost everything else, including role of Yasir Arafat and how much to emphasize security as condition for resuming peace talks with Israel; Powell also meets separately with Egyptian and Jordanian ministers; US urges allies to contribute financially through international agencies, not Palestinian Authority, which Israel says siphons off money for terrorism (M)

2002-07-17.title: India Renews Call for U.S. to Declare Pakistan a Terrorist State
2002-07-17.abstract: India, under intense domestic pressure following deadly attack in Kashmir, renews call for US to declare Pakistan terrorist state (M)

2002-07-17.title: World Briefing | Americas: Paraguay: Protests Taper Off
2002-07-17.abstract: Protesters mostly stay off streets of Asuncion, Paraguay, after declaration of five-day state of emergency to quell demonstrations against economic policies (S)

2002-07-17.title: Spain Arrests 3 Suspects; Tapes of U.S. Sites Seized
2002-07-17.abstract: Police in Spain arrest three men suspected of being operatives of Al Qaeda, and officials say one of them had five-year-old videotapes with hours of surveillance images of World Trade Center and other United States sites that may have been considered as targets; among sites are Disneyland, Universal Studios, Statue of Liberty, Brooklyn Bridge and Golden Gate Brige; two of men are identified as Muhammad Khair al-Saqq and Ghasoub al-Abrash Ghalyoun (aka Abu Musab); their known links with Al Qaeda operations and contents of tapes described (M)

2002-07-18.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: Province Won't Appeal Same-Sex Ruling
2002-07-18.abstract: Ontario provincial government says it will not appeal court ruling that province must extend marriage rights to same-xex couples (S)

2002-07-18.title: World Briefing | Americas: Paraguay: Emergency Lifted
2002-07-18.abstract: Paraguay Pres Luis Gonzalez Macchi lifts state of emergency he decreed on after violent protests against his government's economic policies; rejects demands that he resign, televised speech; links what he calls 'plan of destabilization' to exiled Gen Lino Oviedo and opposition politicians led by Vice Pres Julio Cesar Franco; photo (S)

2002-07-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Suit In Bungled Embryo Case
2002-07-18.abstract: Case in which bungling at fertility clinic left white couple with black twins is taken to Britain's highest court; details are being kept secret by court order, leaving it unclear who is suing whom (S)

2002-07-18.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Reporter's Killers File Appeals
2002-07-18.abstract: Salman Saqib, Fahad Naseem and Shaikh Adil appeal life sentences they face in Pakistan for kidnapping and killing of American reporter Daniel Pearl (S)

2002-07-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Fine On France Sought In Beef Dispute
2002-07-18.abstract: European Commission fines France almost $160,000 for every day it continues to refuse to lift ban on British beef; European Union lifted own ban in 1999, after concluding mad cow disease had been wiped out in Britain (S)

2002-07-19.title: Bush Tells Arab Ministers U.S. Wants Mideast Peace
2002-07-19.abstract: Pres Bush reassures skeptical Arab allies that his administration will press forward with efforts to reach peace in Middle East despite continued attacks on Israel and disagreements over role of Yasir Arafat as Palestinian leader; meets at White House with foreign ministers of Egypt, Jordan and Saudi Arabia (M)

2002-07-19.title: World Briefing | Americas: Argentina: Economy Down, Kidnappings Up
2002-07-19.abstract: Argentine government, responding to growing clamor about increase in violent crime after collapse of economy, announces formation of special antikidnapping brigade (S)

2002-07-19.title: World Briefing | Asia: Philippines: Americans Threatened
2002-07-19.abstract: Abu Sayyaf, militant group in Philippines, warns in radio broadcast that it will attack Americans and American interests in Philippines (S)

2002-07-19.title: Nearly 40 Jewish Graves Are Desecrated in a Rome Cemetery
2002-07-19.abstract: Nearly 40 graves are desecrated in Jewish section of Rome's historic Verano Cemetery; vandals smash headstones, topple columns and overturn earth in chilling scene that marks arrival in Italy of wave of anti-Semitic attacks across Europe; Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi scores 'barbarity of anti-Semitism'; photo (M)

2002-07-19.title: With an Eye On Election, Schröder Fires Defense Chief
2002-07-19.abstract: German Chancellor Gerhard Schroder, underdog for re-election in Sept, dismisses defense min Rudolf Scharping after allegations of financial improprieties (M)

2002-07-20.title: Daddy's Girl Turns Beer-and-TV Billionaire
2002-07-20.abstract: Profile of and interview with Maria Asuncion Aramburuzabala, Mexican who, since death of her father, Pablo, has transformed herself from unemployed, divorced daughter of billionaire into billionaire who manages Grupo Modelo, family fortune built on beer; photo (M)

2002-07-20.title: Reputed Leader of Greek Guerrillas Is Charged in Murders
2002-07-20.abstract: Alexandros Yiotopoulos, mathematician suspected of leading Greek guerrilla group November 17, is charged with murders, bomb attacks and bank robberies carried out during 1980's (M)

2002-07-20.title: U.S. to Punish 10 Businesses For Iran Sales
2002-07-20.abstract: US imposes trade sanctions on nine Chinese companies and Indian businessman for allegedly selling goods or technology to Iran, where they were used by country's chemical and conventional weapons programs; Wisconsin Project on Nuclear Arms Control director Gary Milhollin calls sanctions toothless and urges Congress to enact tougher rules (M)

2002-07-20.title: Israel Threatens to Deport Relatives of 2 Fugitive Militants
2002-07-20.abstract: Israeli Army rounds up and threatens to deport 21 male relatives of Palestinian fugitives Nasser Aseida and Ali Ajouri and destroys their houses; Nasser Aseida is said to be Hamas mastermind of ambush on armored bus in which nine Israelis died and Ali Ajouri is believed to be leader of Fatah-affiliated Aksa Martyrs Brigades who sent two suicide bombers to Tel Aviv, where they killed three men; Palestinian officials and Israeli human rights organizations protest possible deportations; photo; map (M)

2002-07-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: Myanmar: Opposition Seeks Dialogue
2002-07-20.abstract: Myanmar's pro-democracy movement, led by Daw Aung San Suu Kyi, calls for opening of both formal talks and private meetings with government (S)

2002-07-21.title: Midair Crash Opens Debate On Air Safety Over Europe
2002-07-21.abstract: Europe's patchwork of air traffic control systems, major factor in chronic delays in European flights, is now seen as safety threat in wake of July 1 midair collision between Russian passenger jetliner and Boeing 757 cargo plane over southern Germany; among baffling chain of missteps that led to accident was breakdown in communication between Swiss and German controllers; officials are recommending use of emergency phone lines or computer links between air traffic control centers in different countries, and ways of improving communication between pilots and controllers; investigators say German controller tried desperately to call counterpart in Zurich after noticing planes were on collision course, but that line was busy (M)

2002-07-21.title: Flaws in U.S. Air War Left Hundreds of Civilians Dead
2002-07-21.abstract: American air campaign in Afghanistan, based on use of high-tech weaponry, has produced pattern of mistakes that killed hundreds of Afghan civilians; on-site reviews of 11 locations where air strikes killed as many as 400 civilians suggest American commanders sometimes relied on mistaken information from local Afghans; suggest preference for air strikes instead of riskier ground operations eliminated way of checking accuracy of intelligence; indicate Pentagon's use of overwhelming force meant that even when truly military targets were located, civilians were sometimes killed; map; photos (M)

2002-07-21.title: Test Flight Advances Plan for Regular Inter-Korean Air Link
2002-07-21.abstract: North Korean jetliner makes roundtrip test flight to South Korea, first step in opening regular air links between both countries; map; photo (M)

2002-07-21.title: Britain Tries To Ease Enmity Over Kashmir
2002-07-21.abstract: British Foreign Sec Jack Straw, in Islamabad, Pakistan, for talks aimed at easing tensions with India over Kashmir, says he is satisfied with discussions so far (S)

2002-07-21.title: Desperate Palestinians Sneak Into Israel to Work
2002-07-21.abstract: Hundreds of jobless Palestinians are sneaking into Israel illegally with wives and children to work in fields of Tamara and neighboring Israeli Arab communities, despite border closings that have prevented them from reaching previous jobs in Israel for nearly two years and crippled Palestinian economy; photos; maps (M)

2002-07-22.title: Longer-range jets win over Asia
2002-07-22.abstract: SINGAPORE—  For years, passengers flying between Asia and North America across the Pacific have had to put up with the inconvenience and tedium of indirect services.

2002-07-22.title: Sri Lankan Says U.S. Drive on Terror Helps Peace Effort
2002-07-22.abstract: Interview with Prime Min Ranil Wickremesinghe, first Sri Lankan leader to visit Washington in nearly two decades; Wickremesinghe holds that American-led campaign against terrorism had helped his government's chances of ending long civil war; says he will ask for increased aid to rebuild nation's economy; maps; photo (M)

2002-07-22.title: U.S. to Withhold Family-Planning Money From U.N.
2002-07-22.abstract: The Bush administration announced today that it will withhold $34 million because some of the programs' funds have gone to agencies that pressure women to have abortions.

2002-07-22.title: Iran Blew Up Jewish Center In Argentina, Defector Says
2002-07-22.abstract: High-level defector from Iran's intelligence agency says Iranian government organized and carried out bombing of Jewish community center in Buenos Aires, Argentina, in 1994 that killed 85 people and paid former Argentine Pres Carlos Saul Menem $10 million to cover it up; transcript of deposition by Abdolghassem Mesbahi is received by New York Times from Argentine officials frustrated that case remains unsolved; says Menem, president from 1989 to 1999, benefited for years from his ties to Iranian intelligence officials who courted him for his combination of rising political power, Muslim ancestry and connections to Argentina's small but influential Syrian-Lebanese community; allegations in transcript detailed; Menem, through spokesman, rebuts charges; suggests they are politically motivated; has launched new campaign for president despite taint from political corruption scandals; bombing of community center recalled; photos (M)

2002-07-22.title: More Say Yes to Foreign Service, But Not to Hardship Assignments
2002-07-22.abstract: State Dept experiences difficulty filling hardship posts overseas despite record number of people applying to join Foreign Service as American diplomats shun jobs over security and lifestyle concerns; problem is especially acute in Pakistan and Saudi Arabia, whose strategic importance has surged because of US-led campaign against terrorism; 60 percent of embassies and consulates are designated hardship posts for variety of reasons; from Nigeria to Kazakhstan to China, all considered hardship assignments, American missions report vacancy rate 50 percent higher than in more developed nations; while newly minted diplomats are more eager than ever to serve country and even express interest in hardship assignments, they are quick to state desire to avoid places that might pose risk to families; map (M)

2002-07-23.title: Arrests Destroy Noble Image Of Guerrilla Group in Greece
2002-07-23.abstract: Arrest of member of Greece's November 17 guerrilla group shatters image of organization which for generation cast itself as infallible cell of intellectuals, or latter-day Robin Hoods punishing rich on behalf of poor; police may have unmasked most of its major figures, including two real estate agents, state bus driver and French-born mathematician; hold group responsible for 23 killings, including four Americans, since 1975 (M)

2002-07-23.title: U.S. Indicts 5 in Killing of Missionary in Philippines
2002-07-23.abstract: The Justice Dept. announced the indictment today of five suspects in connection with the death of a kidnapped missionary.

2002-07-23.title: Edward Lee Howard, 50, Spy Who Escaped to Soviet Haven
2002-07-23.abstract: Edward Lee Howard, former CIA agent who defected to Soviet Union in mid-1980's after disappearing in New Mexico desert, dies at age 50; photo (M)

2002-07-23.title: World Briefing | Asia: South Korea: Garlic Farmers Protest
2002-07-23.abstract: About 1,000 farmers parading through central Seoul burn copies of secret agreement between South Korea and China that opens way to annual imports of $9 million worth of garlic from China beginning next year (S)

2002-07-23.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Court Refuses Archer Appeal
2002-07-23.abstract: Jeffrey Archer, millionaire author, former Conservative party deputy party leader and member of House of Lords, loses bids before Court of Appeal to reconsider his conviction for perjury and reduce his four-year sentence; photo (S)

2002-07-24.title: BUSH DENOUNCES ISRAELI AIRSTRIKE AS 'HEAVY HANDED'
2002-07-24.abstract: Pres Bush condemns as 'heavy handed' Israeli bombing attack that killed Hamas leader Sheik Salah Shehada and 14 civilians in Gaza; administration says that Israelis knew apartment building hit with one-ton bomb was filled with ordinary Palestinians, including children; Shehada founded military wing of Hamas and was blamed for string of deadly suicide attacks, but scale of damage and televised scenes of infant's body being carried through streets prompt Bush to issue condemnation; photo; Israel says it had no choice and regrets civilian deaths, but military officials, reacting to international criticism, say they did not anticipate extent of collateral damage; top Israeli military envoys met in Washington with Sec Colin Powell and security adviser Condoleezza Rice hours before attack but gave no warning and may not have known (M)

2002-07-24.title: Torture Victims In El Salvador Are Awarded $54 Million
2002-07-24.abstract: Federal jury in Miami orders two retired Salvadoran generals to pay $54.6 million in damages to three civilians who were tortured by security forces during civil war, more than twenty years ago; finds that Jose Guillermo Garcia and Carlos Eugenio Vides Casanova, who now live in Florida, knew of atrocities and did nothing to prevent them; case was brough under 1992 Torture Victims Protection Act (M)

2002-07-24.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Accusation Of Murder In Chechnya
2002-07-24.abstract: International Helsinki Federation for Human Rights charges Russian military conducts deliberate campaign of murder among Chechen civilians, killing between 50 and 80 people a month; says drive seems aimed at young men (S)

2002-07-24.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: A New Look For Tories
2002-07-24.abstract: British Conservative leader Iain Duncan Smith replaces party chairman David Davis with Theresa May, first woman to hold post; is reportedly irked by Davis's resistance to modernizing party; photo (S)

2002-07-24.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-07-24.abstract: IRELAND: 98 Arrests In Immigrant Crackdown; BRITAIN: A New Look For Tories; RUSSIA: Accusation Of Murder In Chechnya; NORTHERN IRELAND: Catholic Quota Upheld;.

2002-07-25.title: World Briefing: Americas
2002-07-25.abstract: ARGENTINA: Menem Acknowledges Swiss Account; PERU: More Charges Against Fujimori; CUBA: Bush Would Veto Easier Travel;.

2002-07-25.title: Blair Says Northern Ireland's Militias Must All Disband
2002-07-25.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair of Britain denounces persistence of violence in Northern Ireland; calls on province's paramilitary groups to disband and warns that government would become more 'rigorous' in punishing them for violating their ceasefire claims; large-scale organized killing has ended, but there has been upsurge of riots, shootings, gasoline bomb attacks and beatings; government believes some of violence is being coordinated by Irish Republican Army and its counterpart Protestant guerrilla groups, all of whom pledged four years ago to maintain ceasefires and abandon systematic mayhem (M)

2002-07-25.title: Young U.S. Catholics Seeking To Put Scandals in Perspective
2002-07-25.abstract: Young American Catholics and church leaders gather in Toronto for first World Youth Day since attention to some priests' victimization of children became crisis for American church; those attending event are willing to acknowledge problem, but not inclined to dwell on it; it is posing little distraction to their celebration of Catholicism as force for good and source of peace; weeklong festival, highlighted by presnce of Pope John Paul II, is being seen as time to move on (M)

2002-07-25.title: U.S. Fails in Effort to Block Vote On U.N. Convention on Torture
2002-07-25.abstract: United States loses bid to rewrite United Nations plan intended to reinforce 1989 convention against torture; Bush administration fears new plan would lead to demands by monitors to gain access to American prisoners and detainees; US sought to create committee in General Assembly to write new plan more acceptable to Washington, but proposal has been defeated; General Assembly will now consider plan approved by UN's Economic and Social Council (M)

2002-07-25.title: Nazi Collaborator Wins Court Challenge on French Trial
2002-07-25.abstract: The European Court of Human Rights ruled today that Maurice Papon was denied a fair trial on charges of war crimes.

2002-07-26.title: World Briefing | Europe: Yugoslavia: Danube Bridge To Be Rebuilt
2002-07-26.abstract: European Union signs $33.8 million agreement to pay for rebuilding main bridge over Danube in Novi Sad, Yugoslavia; it was destroyed in 1999 by NATO bombs during Kosovo war (S)

2002-07-26.title: In Palestinian Children, Signs of Increasing Malnutrition
2002-07-26.abstract: Study under way for United States Agency for International Development is said to be finding that malnutrition among Palestinian children in West Bank and the Gaza Strip has increased substantially during conflict with Israel; preliminary results of study cause alarm within Israeli government and Bush administration, which has been pressuring Israel to alleviate Palestinian suffering; senior Israeli official says study's results could be 'disastrous for Israel'; Israeli government, already anticipating international criticism over conditions among Palestinians, has recently stepped up talks with Palestinian representatives to address needs of those in West Bank; aid workers say few Palestinians can leave their homes to work, and costs and difficulties of transporting goods have become prohibitive; diplomats say Israeli government, concerned about international criticism, has made it easier for international aid groups to distribute food; photo (M)

2002-07-26.title: Deal on Trade Powers Bill
2002-07-26.abstract: Congress reached a tentative pact on a bill to give the White House broader authority to negotiate new trade agreements and increase help for American workers. Page A6.

2002-07-26.title: North Korea Regrets Naval Clash With Seoul and Seeks Talks
2002-07-26.abstract: North Korea issues surprising expression of regret over naval skirmish in Yellow Sea last month in which five South Koreans were killed; calls for cabinet-level talks with South Korea along with steps to revive measures to reconcile two countries; message comes within hours after Pres Kim Dae Jung of South Korea demands apology from North Korea, and day after North warned of more clashes if South Korea and United States insist on their right to block North Korean ships from venturing south of line in Yellow Sea that North Korea has never recognized; analysts say latest message might have been motivated by North Korea's desire for South to ship grain it held back after Yellow Sea skirmish (M)

2002-07-26.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-07-26.abstract: YUGOSLAVIA: Danube Bridge To Be Rebuilt; GREECE: Arrest In Killing Of C.I.A. Chief ; NORTHERN IRELAND: Catholic Funeral; NORTHERN IRELAND: Election Upheld ; RUSSIA: Farms For Sale, For Citizens Only ;.

2002-07-27.title: On the receiving end : Readers go postal in Europe
2002-07-27.abstract: PARIS—  Our report on problems with parcels sent to Europe via the United States Postal Service ("Parcels from U.S. come at high cost," July 4) triggered a wave of correspondence. From France to Italy to Britain to New York, readers wrote of difficulties with the European delivery affiliates of General Logistics Systems, the arm of Consignia that the USPS contracted in October as its single operator for the region.

2002-07-27.title: Powell Hints at Meeting With North Korean
2002-07-27.abstract: Sec Colin Powell, en route to India and Pakistan, says he may meet with North Korean Foreign Min Paek Nam Sun at conference of Southeast Asian nations in Brunei (M)

2002-07-27.title: 'Don't move – I'm coming through'
2002-07-27.abstract: PARIS—  Brad Spurgeon, the IHT's motor racing writer, asked racing drivers from 16 countries for driving tips for visitors:

2002-07-27.title: Property losses and gains : Selling up?Prepare for a taxing time
2002-07-27.abstract: LONDON—  If you are an American expatriate who has bought a home overseas, think carefully before you sell. You may no longer be a homeowner after the transaction is completed, but you may find, to your astonishment, that you have become a shrewd foreign-exchange trader. This unexpected news may come to you from the Internal Revenue Service, and it may cost you dearly.

2002-07-27.title: Swedish Mother's Company Changes the Diaper
2002-07-27.abstract: Profile of Marlene Sandberg of Sweden and her company, Naty AB, which is making small inroads into Europe's highly competitive disposable diaper market; diaper Sandberg developed is 70 percent biodegradable, with more cornstarch and less plastic and other synthetic materials than regular diapers; interview; photo (M)

2002-07-28.title: Sex Charges for 2 New Jersey Priests
2002-07-28.abstract: Police in Montreal arrest two New Jersey priests, Rev William Giblin and Rev Eugene Heyndricks, and charge them with soliciting sex from minors; they and 28 other men are reportedly caught in sting operation meant to break up prostitution ring that included boys as young as 14; Giblin once led Seton Hall Prep (M)

2002-07-28.title: New Zealand's Premier Wins 2nd Term, but Not Total Control
2002-07-28.abstract: Prime Min Helen Clark wins second term for her center-left government in New Zealand, but four conservative opposition parties draw away enough votes to frustrate her effort to win outright parliamentary majority; her Labor Party and Green Party will renew their voting alliance without formal coalition (M)

2002-07-28.title: For Many, Pope's Frailties Now Define Papacy
2002-07-28.abstract: Pope John Paul II's weakened physical condition now largely defines his papacy and overshadows other aspects of it; some Catholics admire pope's courage and determination, but wonder whether he is fit enough to handle challenges that confront him, such as scandal of sexual abuse of children by priests; pope's frailty is clear in Toronto and is said to have been one of reasons he has not included US on this North American visit; photos (M)

2002-07-28.title: Stalemate in Mideast After Deadly Bombing
2002-07-28.abstract: Some Palestinian and Arab leaders contend that Israeli bombing of house of Sheik Salah Shehada in Gaza City, which killed not only Shehada, founder of militant wing of Hamas, but also 14 other people, including 9 children, was deliberate move to block agreement among Palestinian leaders on unilateral cease-fire, or at least lesssening of conflict; Israeli officials scoff at charge, saying that while they knew that Palestinian leaders were engaged in such talks, they also knew from bitter experience that they would go nowhere; photo (M)

2002-07-28.title: Kurds Savor Golden Age That May Prove Fleeting
2002-07-28.abstract: Kurds have created closest thing to free Kurdistan in northern 10th of Iraq, where British and American warplanes have kept Iraqi troops at bay for 10 years, but they fear teir autonomy could be lost if Pres Bush's makes good his threat to oust Saddam Hussein; one fear is that new American war could founder, leaving them exposed to full might of Iraqi reprisals as American troops withdraw, or that Hussein might make pre-emptive strike into Kurdish-controlled areas to keep Americans from using them as base; another fear is that US will succeed in ousting Hussein and that they will be subjected to another dictator, since US has always favored strongman government in Iraq; photos; map (M)

2002-07-29.title: Panel Urges U.S. to Revamp Efforts to Promote Image Abroad
2002-07-29.abstract: Report by Council on Foreign Relations says US is doing poor job of countering growing anti-American sentiment overseas and must revamp way it promotes foreign policies abroad, especially among predominantly Islamic countries; says rising tide of resentment could undermine Bush administration's efforts to fight terrorism and become obstacle if US decides to invade Iraq (M)

2002-07-29.title: Hebron Settlers Attack Palestinian Neighborhood
2002-07-29.abstract: Jewish settlers in Hebron attack Palestinian neighborhood following funeral of Staff Sgt Elazar Leibovich, soldier fatally shot during ambush; kill teenage girl, beat several journalists and battle Israeli police (M)

2002-07-29.title: U.S. Exploring Baghdad Strike As Iraq Option
2002-07-29.abstract: New concept being explored by Bush administration for deposing Iraq's Pres Saddam Hussein offers risky approach of capturing Baghdad and one or two command centers and weapons depots first in hopes of cutting off country's central authority and setting off quick collapse of government; important aim would be to shock Iraq before it could use weapons of mass destruction; 'inside-out' approach would capitalize on American military's ability to strike over long distances, maneuvering forces to envelop target; advocates of plan say it reflects strong desire to find strategy that would not require full quarter-million American troops, yet hits hard enough to succeed (M)

2002-07-29.title: Against a Backdrop of Funerals, Hopeful Hints in Mideast
2002-07-29.abstract: Despite a succession of funerals providing stark evidence of the continuing Arab-Israeli violence, there were scattered signs today of attempts to break the stalemate.

2002-07-29.title: Court Disbands Iranian Party, Sentencing 33 Members to Jail
2002-07-29.abstract: Iran's Revolutionary Court dissolves Iran Freedom Movement, one of oldest opposition parties; sentences 33 of its members to jail while banning others from political activity for as long as 10 years; action is part of new wave of repression to stifle reform movements (S)

2002-07-30.title: World Briefing | Africa: Egypt: 2nd Conviction For Dissident
2002-07-30.abstract: Egyptian-American democracy activist Saad Eddin Ibrahim is convicted of defaming Egypt, other charges, at retrial and sentenced to seven years in prison; Western embassies and rights organizations score verdict; American Embassy expresses concern for his health (S)

2002-07-30.title: Drive by Some in U.S. to Hit Iraq Frustrates Jordan's King
2002-07-30.abstract: Interview with Jordan's King Abdullah II, who expresses frustration with Bush administration's 'fixation' on attacking Iraq; says he despairs at attempts to talk administration hawks out of determination to attack Iraq without moving forward on Mideast peace, which should be top priority (S)

2002-07-30.title: World Briefing
2002-07-30.abstract: ASIANORTH KOREA: READY FOR TALKS, RUSSIAN SAYS After concluding a two-day visit, Foreign Minister Igor S. Ivanov said North Korea was prepared to resume negotiations with the United States and Japan

2002-07-30.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zambia: Ex-Leader's Immunity
2002-07-30.abstract: Opposition Zambian legislators mount high court challenge to court ruling that Parliament lacked authority to strip ex-Pres Frederick Chiluba of immunity from prosecution (S)

2002-07-30.title: Tory M.P. Says He's Gay, Testing British Party's Tolerance
2002-07-30.abstract: Alan Duncan, Tory in British Parliament, tests newly stated commitment by Conservatives to social inclusiveness and tolerance by announcing he is gay; is sent letter of support by party leader Iain Duncan Smith (M)

2002-07-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: Taiwan: Investigators Link Cracks To Crash
2002-07-31.abstract: Investigators say breakup of China Airlines flight on May 25 that killed 225 people apparently began near rear cargo door; say cause is still unknown (S)

2002-07-31.title: Rwanda and Congo Sign Accord to End War
2002-07-31.abstract: Congo Pres Joseph Kabila and Rwanda Pres Paul Kagame sign accord to end war in Congo; South African Pres Thabo Mbeki hosts signing ceremony, Pretoria, South Africa; accord calls for Rwanda to withdraw its troops from large part of eastern Congo controlled by Rwanda and its Congolese rebel allies since war broke out in 1998; Congo agrees to disarm thousands of Hutu militiamen from Rwanda who fled to Congo after perpetrating mass 1994 killings in Rwanda of Tutsi and Hutu moderates; history of conflict and obstacles to peace noted; photo (M)

2002-07-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: Cambodia: Premier Establishes Reserve
2002-07-31.abstract: Cambodian Prime Min Hun Sen sets up million-acre reserve in Cardamom Mountains, filling gap in chain of reserves that together constitute largest unbroken stretch of wildlife habitat left in mainland Southeast Asia; international aid agencies and private conservation groups agreed to pay for maintaining and protecting reserve (S)

2002-07-31.title: Urgent Task For Rumsfeld
2002-07-31.abstract: Analysis of urgent task facing Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld as he presses case for ground action against Iraq to eliminate its weapons of mass destruction; he must make sure American military forces are ready should Pres Bush elect military option in coming months (M)

2002-07-31.title: Powell Treading a Thin Line In Rallying Antiterror Support
2002-07-31.abstract: Sec of State Colin L Powell arrives, Brunei, for Association of Southeast Asian Nations foreign ministers meeting aimed at solidifying Southeast Asian front in drive against global terrorism; has to balance goal with longstanding concerns about human rights abuses in region; says he repeatedly raised rights issues in meetings earlier in day in Malaysia and Singapore; hints at earlier Singapore news conference that Bush administration is ready to discuss resuming military cooperation with Indonesia; met there with Prime Min Goh Chok Tong, who endorsed idea of renewing United States-Indonesia military ties to stabilize Indonesia; met with Prime Min Mahathir Mohamad in Malaysia, where he proposed forming regional training center in Malaysia to coordinate antiterrorism activities; says their talk touched on case of Anwar Ibrahim, whose trial for sodomy and abuse of power Powell says US deems flawed; Asst Sec James Kelly meets with Anwar's wife, Wan Azizah Wan Ismail; photo (M)
